sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cellulitis is a bacterial infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, commonly caused by streptococci or Staphylococcus aureus, usually after a break in the skin.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Erythema, warmth, swelling, and tenderness
- Lymphangitis or regional lymphadenopathy
- Possible discharge or exudate
- Fever, rigors, or sepsis in more severe disease

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Risk is increased by diabetes, lymphoedema, immunosuppression, vascular disease, skin disease, bites, ulcers, and poor self-care
- Facial, periorbital, hand, or joint-related infection needs urgent review
- Many conditions can mimic cellulitis, especially when itch predominates or pain is minimal
- Look for sepsis, abscess, necrotising soft tissue infection, or septic arthritis rather than assuming uncomplicated cellulitis

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Record vital signs, including temperature and signs of systemic illness
- Perform focused assessment for rapidly progressive pain, crepitus, fluctuance, joint involvement, lymphangitis, or disproportionate tenderness
- Consider outlining the affected area to monitor spread
- Consider clinical images, with consent and in line with record policy, if they will assist remote review
- Provide simple analgesia or antipyretic treatment within scope
- Escalate early if sepsis, hand or face involvement, immunocompromise, or concern for deep infection is present

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access if sepsis, dehydration, or IV therapy is likely to be required
- Consider point-of-care blood testing where available and clinically useful
- Consider fluid resuscitation if septic or clinically hypoperfused
- Follow the sepsis pathway if systemic infection is suspected
- Antibiotic therapy should be guided by local protocol and the on-call doctor rather than started reflexively for every skin presentation
- Consider ECG or other adjuncts only if systemic illness or alternative pathology is suspected

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Erysipelas is more superficial and often well demarcated; cellulitis is deeper and more diffuse
- Mimics include contact dermatitis, allergic reaction, venous stasis change, gout, and other inflammatory dermatoses
- Any clinical images must be consented to and handled per record policy
